items.prompt: A function returns the maximum element of a non-empty array. Which input shape requires an explicit contract decision before implementation?

options.text: An array whose values are all equal.

feedback.reason: A maximum is undefined for an empty input unless the contract specifies a sentinel, error, or optional result.
feedback.details: A singleton has an unambiguous maximum: its only value. Increasing and all-equal shapes are also well-defined. Empty input changes the existence of the result itself.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_element: The only element is directly the maximum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.strictly_increasing: The maximum exists and is the final value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_equal: Every value is a valid maximum, so the returned value is still unambiguous.

items.prompt: A function checks whether an array is non-decreasing by comparing adjacent elements. What should it return for an empty array and a one-element array under the usual universal-condition contract?

options.text: false for empty, true for one element.
true for empty, false for one element.

feedback.reason: Neither shape contains an adjacent inversion that violates non-decreasing order.
feedback.details: The property says every adjacent pair must satisfy left <= right. Empty and singleton arrays contain no adjacent pairs, so no counterexample exists.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_false: The absence of pairs does not create a violating pair.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_false_single_true: The same no-counterexample reasoning applies to both shapes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_true_single_false: A one-element array also has no adjacent inversion.

items.prompt: A scan looks for a pair of adjacent equal values. Which input lengths can never contain such a pair?

options.text: Every input length can contain an adjacent equal pair.

feedback.reason: An adjacent pair requires at least two positions.
feedback.details: Length 0 has no values and length 1 has no neighbor. Length 2 can contain a pair, for example [5, 5].

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_or_two: A two-element input can contain exactly one adjacent pair.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_zero: A singleton also lacks a second position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_lengths: Inputs shorter than two elements cannot contain any pair.

items.prompt: A stable deduplication function keeps the first occurrence of every value. Which shape produces the smallest possible logical output for a non-empty input of length n?

options.text: The sequence alternates between two distinct values.

feedback.reason: When all values are equal, stable deduplication retains exactly one occurrence.
feedback.details: All-distinct and strictly increasing inputs retain n values. Alternating two values retain two distinct values. The all-equal shape minimizes output length at one.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: No value can be removed because every occurrence is unique.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.strictly_increasing: Strict increase implies all values are distinct.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.alternating_two_values: Two distinct values remain after stable deduplication.

items.prompt: A filter keeps values greater than zero. What logical output length should be expected when every input value fails the predicate?

feedback.reason: A filter may legally produce an empty result even when the input is non-empty.
feedback.details: The output contains exactly the occurrences satisfying the predicate. If none do, the valid result has length zero.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one: Filtering does not retain a fallback element unless the contract explicitly requires one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n: Retaining all n values would contradict the fact that every value fails.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.undefined: An empty filtered sequence is a well-defined result.

items.prompt: A filter keeps values greater than zero. Which input shape produces a logical output length equal to n?

options.text: Every input value satisfies the predicate.
No input value satisfies the predicate.
Only the first value satisfies the predicate.
Exactly every second value satisfies the predicate.

feedback.reason: The output retains one occurrence for every passing input occurrence.
feedback.details: When all values pass, no position is removed and the logical result length remains n.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_first_passes: This produces a result of length one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.alternating: This retains only a subset of the n positions.

items.prompt: Which shapes should be included when testing a stable deduplication function? Select all that apply.

options.text: Duplicate values are separated by other values.
Only one already sorted, duplicate-free input.

feedback.reason: The test set should exercise no removals, maximal removals, and non-adjacent repetitions.
feedback.details: These shapes expose accidental deletion, incorrect output length, and algorithms that detect only adjacent duplicates.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_sorted_unique: One easy shape cannot reveal behavior under repeated or separated values.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: It verifies that unique values are not removed.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_equal: It verifies maximal duplicate collapse and the minimum non-empty output size.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.separated_duplicates: It catches implementations that confuse adjacent-run compression with global deduplication.

items.prompt: A normalizer removes every punctuation character. What should it return when the input consists entirely of punctuation?

options.text: One punctuation character as a placeholder.
No result, because normalization requires at least one retained character.

feedback.reason: A valid normalization may produce an empty canonical representation.
feedback.details: Every input occurrence belongs to the ignored category, so none is retained. The empty sequence remains a concrete, comparable result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_punctuation: Adding a placeholder introduces data not present in the contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unchanged_input: This fails to remove characters explicitly declared ignorable.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.undefined: An empty canonical result is well-defined unless the contract says otherwise.

items.prompt: A sequence is already in canonical normalized form. Which behavior is expected from an idempotent normalizer?

options.text: The final element is removed to mark completion.
The sequence is always sorted, even when sorting is not part of the contract.

feedback.reason: An idempotent normalizer maps canonical input to itself.
feedback.details: Repeated normalization should not keep changing a representation that already satisfies every canonical rule.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_last: Normalization must not delete meaningful data merely to signal execution.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: Sorting is legal only if order is declared irrelevant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_values: Adding occurrences violates the preservation contract.

items.prompt: A function compares two sequences position by position. Which shape difference can allow an immediate false result before examining corresponding values?

options.text: The sequences have different lengths and equality requires every position to match.
The sequences have the same length.

feedback.details: If lengths differ, one sequence contains an unmatched position regardless of the values in the shared prefix. Equal length is necessary but not sufficient.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_empty: Two empty sequences satisfy positional equality.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_singleton: Their values still need comparison; the shape alone does not prove inequality.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_length: Equal length permits equality but does not determine it.

items.prompt: A function groups consecutive equal values into runs. How many runs are produced by [2, 2, 1, 2, 2]?

feedback.reason: Run boundaries depend on adjacency, so separated occurrences of the same value form different runs.
feedback.details: The runs are [2, 2], [1], and [2, 2]. Global value identity does not merge non-adjacent runs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_runs: This incorrectly merges the two separated runs of value 2.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_run: The value changes twice, creating additional run boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.five_runs: Equal adjacent values belong to the same run rather than separate runs.

items.prompt: Which shapes expose the minimum-length boundary for a three-element window? Select all that apply.

options.text: Length 2, where no legal window exists.
Length 3, where exactly one legal window exists.
Length 4, where two legal starts exist.

feedback.reason: The largest illegal shape, smallest legal shape, and next legal shape reveal the boundary transition.
feedback.details: Lengths 2, 3, and 4 verify zero, one, and multiple window placements. A single large input does not isolate the threshold.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_large_random: A large case may pass despite an off-by-one error at the minimum legal length.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.length_two: It verifies that the algorithm rejects a sequence one element too short.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.length_three: It verifies acceptance of exactly one minimum-size window.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.length_four: It verifies continuation beyond the first legal placement.

items.prompt: Which shapes are useful for testing a frequency-count implementation? Select all that apply.

options.text: One value appears many times while several others appear once.
Only one input where every value appears twice.

feedback.reason: Frequency logic should be tested at minimal key count, maximal key count, and uneven multiplicity.
feedback.details: These shapes expose incorrect initialization, key creation, and repeated increment behavior.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_one_balanced_case: One symmetric case does not test extremes or skewed distributions.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_same: It verifies repeated updates to one key and the minimum distinct-key count.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: It verifies creation of one count entry per occurrence and the maximum distinct-key count.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.one_dominant_value: It checks that large and small counts coexist correctly.

items.prompt: An adjacent scan examines every pair in an array of length n. Select the number of pair checks and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: The algorithm uses only a constant number of local variables.

feedback.reason: Sequence length determines the number of adjacent boundaries, including the zero-pair edge cases.
feedback.details: Lengths 0 and 1 produce zero checks. For n >= 2, each boundary between neighboring elements is examined once, yielding n - 1 checks.

items.prompt: A developer sorts an input before checking whether the original sequence alternates between two values. What is the main defect?

options.text: Sorting destroys the original positional shape that defines alternation.
Sorting is unable to process sequences containing exactly two values.
Sorting is invalid because it always uses constant space.

feedback.reason: Alternation is defined by the original order of neighboring positions.
feedback.details: Sorting groups equal values and can turn an alternating sequence into runs. The multiset remains similar, but the shape relevant to the question is lost.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_removes_duplicates: Sorting rearranges occurrences but does not delete them.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_cannot_handle_two_values: Sorting can process such inputs; it simply violates the positional contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_constant_space: Space usage is not the semantic defect.

items.prompt: A function removes only consecutive duplicates. What should it return for [1, 2, 1]?

feedback.reason: The repeated 1 values are separated, so the sequence contains no consecutive duplicate run.
feedback.details: The input shape matters: global repetition does not imply adjacent repetition. Every original occurrence remains under the consecutive-only contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.global_unique: This applies global deduplication rather than consecutive-run collapse.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_only: This removes distinct and non-consecutive values without justification.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_unique: Sorting is not implied and would change the original sequence shape.
